mechanism: Periglandula-associated symbiosis contributes ergoline alkaloid production in seeds; mechanistic findings are preclinical and do not establish clinical benefit.
effects: Reports emphasize psychoactive/hallucinogenic potential linked to ergoline alkaloids.
Direct therapeutic efficacy claims remain unsupported by robust controlled human trials in reviewed sources.
contraindications: Avoid in people with psychosis risk, bipolar-spectrum instability, or severe anxiety vulnerability.
Avoid in pregnancy/breastfeeding because safety has not been established.
safetyNotes: Potential for acute psychological distress, dysphoria, or intoxication-related harm.
Product variability and co-exposures increase unpredictability of effects.
description: Ipomoea tricolor seeds are historically associated with ritual psychoactive use in parts of Mesoamerica. Modern literature mainly addresses fungal symbiosis and ergoline alkaloid biosynthesis.
dosage: No validated therapeutic dosage context was identified in the approved source set.
interactions: Potential additive psychoactive effects with serotonergic or CNS-active substances.
preparation: Historical use centers on seed preparations in ceremonial settings; modern evidence does not support unsupervised therapeutic ingestion.
region: Cultivated globally; traditional ritual contexts are most documented in Mesoamerican settings.
traditionalUse: Traditional ceremonial use is described in ethnobotanical records; this should remain separated from clinical-efficacy claims.
summary: Ipomoea tricolor (morning glory) is discussed for ergoline-associated chemistry linked to hallucinogenic potential; evidence is primarily preclinical/mechanistic with limited direct human therapeutic data.
